tier_1_regimens.site: Primary locally advanced MCC (N0) when curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Data from non-randomized trials demonstrate improved durable response rates with PD-1/PD-L1 blockade compared to cytotoxic therapy. Avelumab is an anti-PD-L1 monoclonal antibody. Real-world outcomes include ORR 73%, median PFS 24.4 months, and median OS 30.7 months [1,2]. Four-year OS rate was 38%; PD-L1 positive tumors had longer median OS (38.7 months) vs. PD-L1 negative (16.1 months) [3].

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary locally advanced MCC (N0) when curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab. Complete response rate was 30% [4].

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary locally advanced MCC (N0) when curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: For chemotherapy-naïve patients, complete response was 12.3% and disease control rate was 53.8% [5].

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary locally advanced MCC (N0) when curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients who are surgical candidates. Pathologic complete response (pCR) was 47.2% in 36 patients who underwent surgery. 54.5% of radiographically evaluable patients had tumor reductions ≥30% [6]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV Nivolumab.

tier_1_regimens.site: Recurrent locally advanced MCC (N0) when curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.site: Recurrent locally advanced MCC (N0) when curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: For chemotherapy-naïve patients [5].

tier_1_regimens.notes: Real-world outcomes include ORR 73%, median PFS 24.4 months, and median OS 30.7 months [1,2].

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary regional disease (N+) if curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV Nivolumab. In treatment-naïve patients, complete response was 21%, partial response 50% [7].

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary regional disease (N+) if curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [4].

tier_1_regimens.site: Primary regional disease (N+) if curative surgery and curative RT not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab is particularly indicated for disease that is refractory to PD-1 therapy. Complete response rate was 41% in ICI-naïve patients [8,9]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is not approved for concurrent use with IV Ipilimumab.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare and aggressive primary cutaneous neuroendocrine neoplasm. It originates from Merkel cells, which are mechanoreceptor cells located in the basal layer of the epidermis and are thought to derive from the ectoderm. MCC is characterized by small, round, blue cells with neuroendocrine features, often displaying high mitotic activity and dense-core granules on electron microscopy. Anatomically, MCC arises in the skin, most commonly on sun-exposed areas such as the head and neck (approximately 50% of cases) and extremities. However, it can occur at virtually any cutaneous site. The disease is clinically significant due to its rapid growth potential and high propensity for early lymphatic and hematogenous metastasis. The primary tumor often presents as a painless, firm, red-to-violaceous nodule or plaque, which may be mistaken clinically for a benign cyst or other skin lesion. Histopathological confirmation requires immunohistochemistry (IHC) to distinguish MCC from cutaneous simulants and metastatic tumors, with key diagnostic markers including positive cytokeratin 20 (CK20) staining in a characteristic membranous and/or paranuclear dot-like pattern and negative thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF-1) expression. The disease is staged using the AJCC 8th edition TNM system, which categorizes tumors based on primary tumor size and depth of invasion, regional lymph node involvement, and distant metastasis. MCC is associated with a poor prognosis, with 5-year survival rates ranging from 41% to 77% depending on stage at presentation, underscoring its status as one of the most lethal skin cancers, with a mortality rate exceeding that of melanoma.

subtypes.name: Virus-Associated (Merkel Cell Polyomavirus-Positive) MCC
subtypes.description: This subtype is characterized by the clonal integration of Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) into the tumor genome. The virus is detected in 43% to 100% of MCC tumors. MCPyV-positive MCC tumors typically arise in immunosuppressed individuals and on sun-exposed skin, often the head and neck. They generally exhibit a lower mutational burden compared to virus-negative tumors.
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with immunosuppression (e.g., organ transplant recipients, chronic lymphocytic leukemia [CLL], HIV infection). The viral Large T antigen inactivates tumor suppressor proteins p53 and retinoblastoma protein (pRb). Prognostic impact is variable; some studies suggest improved survival, while others show no significant difference.

subtypes.name: UV-Associated (Merkel Cell Polyomavirus-Negative) MCC
subtypes.description: This subtype is not associated with MCPyV and is strongly linked to ultraviolet (UV) radiation-induced DNA damage. Genetic analyses reveal a high mutational burden, with a distinctive signature of cytosine to thymine (C to T) transitions indicative of UV damage. These tumors often have mutations in tumor suppressor genes such as TP53 and RB1.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for the remaining proportion of MCC cases (up to 57%).
subtypes.key_associations: More likely to occur on the head, neck, or trunk. Some studies associate this subtype with increased recurrence, decreased MCC-specific survival, and decreased overall survival compared to virus-positive tumors. The higher mutational burden may make these tumors more responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitors.

subtypes.name: Combined/Composite MCC
subtypes.description: A histologic variant where MCC is found concurrently with another primary skin cancer, most commonly squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), within the same pathologic specimen. Less frequently, it can be combined with basal cell carcinoma (BCC) or melanoma.
subtypes.frequency: Relatively rare; exact frequency not specified in the source.
subtypes.key_associations: Patients with combined tumors may be more likely to have a history of prior non-melanoma skin cancers, non-hematologic extracutaneous cancers, and immunosuppression. The prognostic significance of the combined phenotype is uncertain due to limited comparative data.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is largely bifurcated based on the presence or absence of Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV). In virus-positive MCC, which comprises 43% to 100% of cases, the clonal integration of MCPyV into the host genome is an early oncogenic event. The virus expresses truncated forms of the Large T antigen and small T antigen. These oncoproteins drive proliferation by inactivating key tumor suppressor proteins: the Large T antigen binds to and inhibits retinoblastoma protein (pRb), while both antigens can interfere with p53 pathway function. This viral-driven model results in a tumor with a relatively low mutational burden. In contrast, virus-negative MCC arises through a UV radiation-dependent pathway, evidenced by a high mutational burden and a characteristic UV signature (cytosine to thymine transitions). Genetic analyses of these tumors frequently show mutations in tumor suppressor genes, including TP53 and RB1. Additionally, alterations in other cancer-associated genes have been reported. Genomic studies have noted that approximately 20% of patients under 50 years of age with MCC may harbor germline mutations in known cancer-associated genes, suggesting a potential heritable component in early-onset cases. The distinct molecular pathways contribute to the biological heterogeneity of MCC, with virus-negative tumors being more heavily mutated and potentially more susceptible to immunotherapies like PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors. The high expression of programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) in some MCC tumors, regardless of viral status, provides a rationale for the efficacy of checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is a rare but increasingly common and aggressive skin cancer. In the United States, the estimated annual incidence is approximately 2,488 individuals. The incidence has been rising dramatically over recent decades, with an estimated increase of over 300% between 2000 and 2013, and it is projected to continue rising due to an aging population, increased sun exposure, improved diagnostic recognition, and a growing immunocompromised population. MCC primarily affects older adults, with the median age at diagnosis typically in the late 70s. The disease shows a slight male predominance, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 1.5:1 to 2:1. Racial disparities are significant: MCC is far more common in white individuals compared to other ethnicities. The incidence is highest in populations with lighter skin tones and in geographic areas with high ultraviolet (UV) radiation indices, such as Australia and the southwestern United States. There is a strong association with immunosuppression; the risk is 5 to 10 times higher in organ transplant recipients and is also elevated in patients with hematologic malignancies like chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and those with HIV infection. Mortality is high, with MCC carrying a mortality rate that exceeds that of melanoma. Five-year relative or MCC-specific survival rates range widely from 41% to 77% depending on the stage at diagnosis, highlighting the aggressive natural history of the disease.

risk_factors.detail: Sun exposure is considered a major risk factor. This is supported by the increased incidence in geographic areas with higher UV indices, the tendency for MCC to arise on sun-exposed skin, and the frequent co-occurrence with other UV-induced skin lesions. In virus-negative MCC, tumors exhibit a UV-specific mutational signature (C to T transitions).

MCC is disproportionately more common in immunocompromised individuals. This includes solid organ transplant recipients, patients with hematologic malignancies (especially chronic lymphocytic leukemia [CLL]), and individuals with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection. Immunosuppression is a risk factor for poor outcomes and increased recurrence.
risk_factors.strength: Strong clinical association; risk is 5-10 times higher in transplant recipients.

risk_factors.detail: Clonal integration of MCPyV into tumor cell DNA is found in 43% to 100% of MCC cases. The virus is considered a cofactor in the pathogenesis of a subset of MCC tumors, though its precise role in causation vs. passenger is under study.
risk_factors.strength: Strong molecular evidence linking virus to tumor biology.

risk_factors.detail: The incidence of MCC increases significantly with age, with the vast majority of cases diagnosed in individuals over 50 years old.

risk_factors.factor: Fair Skin and Light Hair Color
risk_factors.detail: Individuals with lighter skin phototypes (Fitzpatrick types I and II) are at substantially higher risk, consistent with increased susceptibility to UV radiation damage.

Mutations in known cancer-associated genes that may be heritable have been reported in approximately 20% of patients with MCC under 50 years of age, suggesting a potential genetic predisposition in a subset of early-onset cases.
risk_factors.strength: Emerging evidence; cited in updated guidelines.

management_principles.overview: Merkel Cell Carcinoma (MCC) is an aggressive cutaneous neuroendocrine neoplasm with high rates of locoregional recurrence and distant metastasis. Treatment philosophy is multimodal, with surgery being the primary treatment for resectable disease, supplemented by adjuvant radiation therapy (RT) based on risk factors. Systemic therapy, particularly immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), is central for advanced, inoperable, or metastatic disease. A multidisciplinary approach is universally recommended due to the rarity and complexity of MCC [1]. The management paradigm has shifted with the advent of ICIs (avelumab, pembrolizumab, nivolumab, retifanlimab-dlwr), which demonstrate improved durable response rates and survival compared to historical cytotoxic chemotherapy [2-5]. Treatment decisions are heavily influenced by disease stage, patient performance status, comorbidities (especially immunosuppression), and anatomic location.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with disease limited to the primary tumor, no in-transit, nodal, or distant metastases.
by_intent.approach: Surgical excision is the cornerstone. The extent of adjuvant RT is guided by surgical margin status and presence of adverse risk factors (lymphovascular invasion [LVI], immunosuppression, tumor >1 cm, close/positive margins, positive sentinel lymph node biopsy [SLNB], head/neck primary). For patients who are surgical candidates, SLNB is an important staging tool. Neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered for select patients with locally advanced disease to potentially convert to resectability [3].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with clinically localized disease for whom curative surgery and/or adjuvant RT are not feasible due to comorbidities or tumor characteristics.
by_intent.approach: Multidisciplinary consultation is required. For surgical candidates, neoadjuvant anti-PD-1 therapy (nivolumab) is considered to assess response and enable subsequent curative local therapy. For non-surgical candidates, a discussion between definitive RT for durable local control versus systemic therapy is recommended [MCC-3].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with nodal metastases (regional disease) or in-transit metastases (AJCC N2/N3).
by_intent.approach: Management of the primary tumor follows the localized pathway. For the nodal basin, node dissection plus RT is preferred. For in-transit disease, options include clinical trial, surgery, RT, or systemic therapy. Additional local considerations (T-VEC, hyperthermic isolated limb perfusion/infusion) are useful in certain circumstances [MCC-4].

Clinical trial enrollment is preferred. Systemic therapy with ICIs is the standard approach. RT and/or surgery may be considered in highly selective circumstances for oligometastases or symptom control. Best supportive care is an option [MCC-5].

by_intent.patient_group: All treated patients in follow-up; patients with recurrence.
by_intent.approach: Regular clinical follow-up with skin and lymph node exams is recommended. Imaging and biomarker testing (MCPyV antibodies, ctDNA) are performed as clinically indicated, with routine imaging surveillance considered for high-risk patients. Treatment of recurrence follows the pathway for the respective recurrent stage (e.g., regional recurrence treated per N+ pathway) [MCC-6].

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise is recommended prior to the start of primary treatment for all clinical stages (N0, N+, M1) [MCC-1, MCC-4, MCC-5]. This is especially critical for complex cases, locally advanced disease, in-transit disease, and metastatic disease to ensure optimal treatment planning.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: The guideline does not provide explicit performance status thresholds. However, the recommendation for systemic therapy with ICIs and the discussion of treatment goals (curative vs. palliative) imply that patients should have adequate performance status to tolerate proposed therapies. The approach for patients unfit for aggressive local or systemic therapy includes consideration of best supportive care alone [MCC-5].

management_pathways.title: Clinical N0 Disease: Local MCC Only, Surgically Resectable
branching_basis: Presence of adverse risk factors for local recurrence

options.label: Primary Excision
options.recommendation: Excision with 1- to 2-cm margins, or Mohs/peripheral and deep en face margin assessment (PDEMA) in certain circumstances [MCC-2, footnote i, j]. SLNB with appropriate immunopanel should be performed prior to or at the time of definitive excision [MCC-2, footnote h].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant treatment is determined by pathology results. Key factors: margin status, SLNB result, and adverse risk factors (LVI, immunosuppression, primary tumor >1 cm, close/positive margins, head/neck site).

nodes.criteria: Pathology: Clear margins AND No adverse risk factors

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: Observation is recommended for the primary site and the nodal basin if SLNB is negative [MCC-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy recommended.

nodes.criteria: Pathology: Clear margins AND ≥1 adverse risk factors

options.label: Adjuvant RT to Primary Tumor Site
options.recommendation: Adjuvant RT is recommended [MCC-2, footnote n]. If adjuvant RT is planned, narrower clinical margins are acceptable with the goal of clear or microscopically positive margins [MCC-2, footnote l].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT recommended due to high risk of local recurrence.

options.label: Adjuvant RT to Primary Tumor Site
options.recommendation: Adjuvant RT is the preferred additional treatment [MCC-2]. Re-excision may be considered if RT is not feasible or planned.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT preferred over re-excision.

nodes.criteria: Pathology: Grossly positive margins and further resection not possible

options.label: Adjuvant RT to Primary Tumor Site
options.recommendation: Adjuvant RT is recommended [MCC-2, footnote n].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT recommended.

options.label: Baseline Imaging
options.recommendation: Baseline imaging via whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or CT with contrast of chest, abdomen, pelvis, and neck (if head/neck primary) [MCC-2, footnote m]. MRI of the brain if clinical suspicion of brain metastases or direct extension.

options.label: Node Dissection
options.recommendation: Therapeutic lymph node dissection (TLND) is an option for the involved nodal basin [MCC-2].

options.label: Adjuvant RT to Nodal Basin
options.recommendation: RT to the nodal basin is a preferred option, especially following node dissection with multiple involved nodes and/or extranodal extension (ENE) [MCC-2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management of the positive SLN is required, typically with TLND and/or RT to the nodal basin.

options.label: Observation of Nodal Basin
options.recommendation: Observation is the preferred management [MCC-2].

options.label: Consider RT to Nodal Basin
options.recommendation: May consider RT to the nodal basin for certain high-risk clinical scenarios (e.g., compromised SLNB, high risk of false-negative, profound immunosuppression) [MCC-2, footnote p].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation standard; RT considered only in specific high-risk situations.

management_pathways.title: Clinical N0 Disease: Locally Advanced MCC When Curative Surgery and RT Are Not Feasible

nodes.criteria: Clinical N0, locally advanced MCC, surgical candidate

options.label: Neoadjuvant Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered [MCC-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: This is a neoadjuvant approach to potentially enable subsequent surgery/RT.

options.label: Proceed to Surgery (MCC-2)
options.recommendation: Patient becomes eligible for the primary and additional treatment options for patients with surgically resectable local MCC (Pathway: localized_resectable_clinical_n0).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Follow standard pathway for resectable disease post-neoadjuvant therapy.

options.label: Discuss RT for Local Control
options.recommendation: Discuss RT for durable local control versus systemic therapy [MCC-3].

options.label: Discuss Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Discuss systemic therapy as an alternative to local therapy [MCC-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Recurrent/progressive disease after neoadjuvant ICI.

nodes.criteria: Clinical N0, locally advanced MCC, non-surgical candidate (due to comorbidities or tumor characteristics)

options.label: Discuss RT for Durable Local Control
options.recommendation: Discuss RT for durable local control versus systemic therapy [MCC-3].

options.label: Discuss Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Discuss systemic therapy versus RT [MCC-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative-intent local or systemic therapy discussion.

management_pathways.title: Clinical N+ Disease: Regional MCC / In-transit Disease
branching_basis: Feasibility of curative surgery and/or RT

options.label: FNA or Core Biopsy with Immunopanel
options.recommendation: Confirm diagnosis with fine-needle aspiration (FNA) or core biopsy with appropriate immunopanel [MCC-4, footnote a].

options.label: Excisional Biopsy
options.recommendation: An excisional biopsy may be considered to confirm a negative initial FNA or core LN biopsy if clinical suspicion remains high [MCC-4, footnote s].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Initial diagnostic workup.

options.label: Node Dissection + RT
options.recommendation: Node dissection and RT is the preferred management of the draining nodal basin [MCC-4].

options.label: Node Dissection OR RT
options.recommendation: Node dissection or RT is an alternative [MCC-4].

options.label: Clinical Trial
options.recommendation: Clinical trial is preferred if available [MCC-4].

options.label: Consider Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Consider systemic therapy [MCC-4, footnote r].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Multimodal treatment of the regional basin is standard.

options.label: Surgery or RT for Primary
options.recommendation: Primary tumor management follows localized pathways (MCC-2/MCC-3).

options.label: Consider Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Consider systemic therapy [MCC-4, footnote r].

options.label: Radiographic Surveillance
options.recommendation: Radiographic surveillance is an option if initial biopsy was negative [MCC-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management based on primary tumor status and surveillance results.

options.label: Clinical Trial
options.recommendation: Clinical trial is preferred [MCC-4].

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Surgery is an option [MCC-4].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: RT is an option [MCC-4, footnote n].

options.label: Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is an option [MCC-4, footnote r].

options.label: Additional Local Considerations
options.recommendation: Additional local considerations if curative surgery and/or RT not feasible: T-VEC (intralesional talimogene laherparepvec) for palliation of injectable sites [MCC-4, footnote r].

options.label: Hyperthermic Isolated Limb Infusion/Perfusion
options.recommendation: Hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion [MCC-4, footnote r, Thiels CA, et al. 2016].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment of in-transit metastases; options mirror regional/metastatic disease with added local therapies.

options.label: Clinical Trial
options.recommendation: Clinical trial preferred if available [MCC-5].

options.label: Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Consider any of the following therapies or combinations of: Systemic therapy [MCC-5, footnote r].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: Consider RT, often for palliation or oligometastatic disease [MCC-5, footnote n, o].

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Consider surgery under highly selective circumstances (e.g., oligometastases) in the context of multidisciplinary consultation [MCC-5, footnote u].

options.label: Best Supportive Care
options.recommendation: Best supportive care (See NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care) [MCC-5].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment intent is typically palliative, but cure may be possible in oligometastatic settings with aggressive local and systemic therapy.

management_pathways.title: Follow-up, Recurrence, and Treatment
branching_basis: Type of recurrence (local, regional, in-transit, distant)

options.label: Physical Exam
options.recommendation: Physical exam including complete skin and complete LN exam: Every 3-6 months for 3 years, then every 6-12 months thereafter [MCC-6].

options.label: Imaging as Clinically Indicated
options.recommendation: Imaging and other studies as clinically indicated (e.g., for symptoms, rising biomarkers) [MCC-6, footnote c, d, v].

options.label: Routine Imaging Surveillance
options.recommendation: Recommend routine imaging surveillance for patients at high risk (e.g., stage IIIB+, immunosuppression, non-MCPyV) [MCC-6, footnote w].

options.label: MCPyV Antibodies and/or ctDNA
options.recommendation: Quantitation of serum MCPyV oncoprotein antibodies and/or ctDNA may be considered, often every 3 months [MCC-1, footnote c, d; MCC-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Active surveillance protocol to detect recurrence early.

options.label: Clinical Trial
options.recommendation: Clinical trial preferred if available [MCC-6].

options.label: Systemic Therapy
options.recommendation: Consider systemic therapy [MCC-6, footnote r].

options.label: RT
options.recommendation: Consider RT [MCC-6, footnote n, o].

options.label: Surgery
options.recommendation: Consider surgery [MCC-6, footnote u].

options.label: Best Supportive Care
options.recommendation: Best supportive care [MCC-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment approach mirrors de novo stage-appropriate disease (e.g., local recurrence may follow localized pathways).

options.label: Follow Clinical N+ / In-transit Pathway (MCC-4)
options.recommendation: Treat per the Clinical N+ or In-transit disease pathway (MCC-4) [MCC-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management follows the regional recurrence pathway.

options.label: Follow Treatment of M1 Disease Pathway (MCC-5)
options.recommendation: Treat per the M1 disease pathway (MCC-5) [MCC-6].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Management follows the metastatic disease pathway.

items.text: Complete history and physical (H&P) examination, including thorough skin and lymph node examination.
Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise.
items.recommendation: Recommended for all patients prior to starting primary treatment.

items.text: Biopsy of primary tumor with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) staining and immunopanel (CK20, TTF-1).
items.recommendation: Mandatory for diagnosis. Additional neuroendocrine markers (INSM1, synaptophysin, chromogranin, CD56, NSE) may be used if atypical staining pattern is present [MCC-1, footnote a; MCC-A].

items.text: Consider collecting additional tumor tissue if diagnosis was made only with a core biopsy, to enable tissue-informed ctDNA testing.
items.recommendation: Recommended prior to immunotherapy or RT [MCC-1].

items.text: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) OR CT with contrast of chest, abdomen, pelvis, and neck (if primary on head/neck).
items.recommendation: Encouraged for staging of most cases due to high rate of occult metastases (12-20%) [MCC-1, footnote b]. MRI of the brain with and without contrast if clinical suspicion of brain metastases or direct extension.

items.text: Serum Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) oncoprotein antibodies.
items.recommendation: May be considered as part of initial workup. Seronegativity confers higher recurrence risk; seropositivity with rising titer can indicate recurrence. Baseline within 3 months of treatment [MCC-1, footnote c].
Can assess disease burden in virus-positive and virus-negative MCC. Often obtained every 3 months for surveillance [MCC-1, footnote d].

items.text: Laboratory studies as clinically indicated (e.g., CBC, CMP, LFTs).
items.recommendation: To assess general health and organ function.

items.text: Assessment of immunosuppression status (e.g., history of organ transplant, HIV, CLL, chronic steroid use).
items.recommendation: Critical, as immunosuppression is a risk factor for poor outcomes. Minimize immunosuppressive treatments as clinically feasible [MCC-1, footnote e; MCC-6, footnote e].

items.text: Consider genetic counseling to evaluate for germline mutations in cancer-associated genes.
items.recommendation: Recommended for patients <50 years of age [MCC-1, footnote f].

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary treatment for most localized and resectable MCC. It is essential for both treatment and accurate pathologic staging (primary tumor and SLNB).
principles: Obtain histologically negative margins when clinically feasible, balancing oncologic goals with surgical morbidity [MCC-C].
Coordinate surgical management so that SLNB is performed prior to or at the time of definitive excision [MCC-C, footnote a].
Primary tissue closure should be the goal to avoid undue delay in proceeding to adjuvant RT if needed, as delay >7-8 weeks is associated with worse outcomes [MCC-2, footnote i; MCC-B, footnote 3].

procedures.name: Wide Local Excision (WLE)
procedures.indication: Standard approach for primary tumor excision.
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, infection, wound dehiscence, cosmetic/functional morbidity, lymphedema if near nodal basin.

procedures.name: Mohs Micrographic Surgery or other forms of Peripheral and Deep En Face Margin Assessment (PDEMA)
procedures.approach: Staged excision with intraoperative margin assessment, tissue conservation.
procedures.indication: May be appropriate in certain circumstances, especially for cosmetically sensitive areas, to ensure clear margins while sparing tissue. Must not interfere with SLNB [MCC-2, footnote j; MCC-C].
procedures.key_complications: Similar to WLE; requires specialized pathologist training.

procedures.name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)
procedures.approach: Identification and excision of sentinel lymph node(s) with immunopanel staining.
procedures.indication: Important staging tool for clinically node-negative disease. Recommended prior to or at the time of primary excision [MCC-C, footnote a].
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema, seroma, nerve injury, false-negative results (reported rates 17-21%).

procedures.name: Therapeutic Lymph Node Dissection (TLND)
procedures.approach: Surgical removal of regional lymph nodes.
procedures.indication: For biopsy-proven regional nodal metastases. Part of preferred management of the draining nodal basin (node dissection + RT) [MCC-4].
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema (significant risk, especially groin), seroma, nerve injury, infection.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Disease is considered resectable when complete extirpation of the primary tumor is surgically feasible with acceptable morbidity. The term 'locally advanced' is used when curative surgery is not feasible due to comorbidities or tumor characteristics [MCC-3]. For nodal disease, resectability is assessed in context of multidisciplinary discussion.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Optimize patient fitness for surgery and anesthesia. Minimize immunosuppressive medications where possible (e.g., adjust immunosuppressive regimen in transplant patients in consultation with managing physician) [MCC-1, footnote e; MCC-6, footnote e]. Ensure multidisciplinary consultation has occurred.

radiation_therapy.role: RT plays a critical adjuvant role for locoregional control, especially in the presence of high-risk features. It is also used as definitive local therapy for inoperable primary tumors or as palliative therapy for metastatic disease.
principles: Expeditious initiation of adjuvant RT after surgery is preferred as soon as wound healing permits, as delay >7 to 8 weeks has been associated with worse outcomes [MCC-B, footnote 3].
Appropriateness of RT should be determined together with a radiation oncologist [MCC-2, footnote o].
Recommendations are based on clinical practice at NCCN Member Institutions and evidence from other skin cancers due to limited MCC-specific data [MCC-B].
IGRT is best practice when using IMRT, proton beam, or 3D conformal RT. Use of bolus over primary tumor should be considered to ensure full surface dose [MCC-B].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT to Primary Tumor Site
dose_frameworks.indication: After excision with negative (50-56 Gy), microscopically positive (56-60 Gy), or grossly positive (60 Gy) margins. Hypofractionation options include 2.2-2.5 Gy x 20, 3-3.5 Gy x 10, or single 8 Gy fraction if not candidate for protracted course [MCC-B, page 2].

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT to Draining Nodal Basin
dose_frameworks.indication: After SLNB positive (50-56 Gy), after LN dissection with multiple involved nodes and/or ENE (50-60 Gy). Hypofractionation options include 2.2-2.5 Gy x 20, 3-3.5 Gy x 10, or 8 Gy x 2 fractions [MCC-B, page 4].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT to Clinically Evident Primary Tumor
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: 1.8-2 Gy (conventional); 2.5 Gy, 3 Gy, 4 Gy, 6-7 Gy, 8 Gy (hypofractionated)
dose_frameworks.indication: For unresectable primary tumor, surgery refused, or surgery would result in significant morbidity. Hypofractionation regimens include 2.5 Gy x 20, 3 Gy x 15-17, 4 Gy x 10, 6-7 Gy x 5, or 8 Gy x 3 [MCC-B, page 2].

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive RT to Clinically Evident Nodal Disease
dose_frameworks.indication: For clinically evident lymphadenopathy (60 Gy), or clinically node-negative but at risk for subclinical disease (46-50 Gy). Hypofractionation includes 2.5 Gy x 20, 3 Gy x 15-17, or 6-7 Gy x 5 fractions (2 treatments/week) [MCC-B, page 4].

approaches.name: Adjuvant RT for High-Risk Localized Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: Conventional (1.8-2 Gy/fx to 50-60 Gy) or hypofractionated schedules as above.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not routinely recommended. Postoperative chemoradiation may have a role in particularly high-risk MCCs with residual disease [MS-20].
approaches.indication: After surgery for primary tumor with ≥1 adverse risk factors (LVI, immunosuppression, >1 cm tumor, close/positive margins, positive SLNB, head/neck site) or after node dissection with multiple involved nodes and/or ENE [MCC-2].
approaches.key_trial: Retrospective data support improved local control and OS with adjuvant RT [21, 68, 264].
approaches.toxicities: Radiation dermatitis, fatigue, fibrosis, lymphedema (if treating nodal basin), xerostomia (head/neck).

approaches.name: Definitive RT for Inoperable Primary or Nodal Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: Conventional (56-60 Gy) or various hypofractionated schedules as above.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Consider for select patients, but evidence is limited.
approaches.indication: Primary tumor not amenable to surgery due to comorbidities or tumor characteristics, or patient refusal. Also for clinically evident nodal disease where surgery is not planned.
approaches.key_trial: Retrospective data show lower outcomes than surgery but better than no local therapy. 5-year DSS 73% with RT vs 54% without in non-surgical patients [13].
approaches.toxicities: Similar to adjuvant RT, potentially higher with larger treatment volumes.

approaches.name: Palliative RT
approaches.dose_fractionation: Hypofractionated: 3 Gy x 10, 4 Gy x 5, or 8 Gy x 1-3 fractions.
approaches.indication: Symptomatic metastatic sites, bleeding, pain, or obstruction.
approaches.key_trial: Not specified; based on general palliative RT principles.
approaches.toxicities: Acute: pain, skin reaction, fatigue. Late: fibrosis, organ dysfunction depending on site.

systemic_therapy.overview: Data from non-randomized trials demonstrate improved durable response rates with PD-1/PD-L1 blockade compared with cytotoxic therapy. The safety profiles are significantly different, requiring clinician and patient education for managing immune-related adverse events [MCC-D, footnote b]. Clinical trial enrollment is preferred when available.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Primary Locally Advanced Disease (Clinical N0, if curative surgery and curative RT not feasible)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 2A recommendations based on improved durable responses with ICIs over historical chemotherapy [MCC-D 2 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other Recommended: Retifanlimab-dlwr. Useful in Certain Circumstances: Neoadjuvant Nivolumab (for surgical candidates) [MCC-D 2 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirement stated for first-line use.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Recurrent Locally Advanced Disease (Clinical N0, if curative surgery and curative RT not feasible)
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirement stated for first-line use.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Primary Regional Disease (Clinical N+, if curative surgery and curative RT not feasible)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 2A recommendations for ICIs in incurable regional disease [MCC-D 3 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other Recommended: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (particularly for disease refractory to PD-1 therapy [footnote g]). Useful in Certain Circumstances: Cytotoxic chemotherapy if anti-PD-L1 or anti-PD-1 therapy is contraindicated or progressed [MCC-D 3 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: No specific biomarker requirement stated for first-line use.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Recurrent Regional Disease (Clinical N+) and In-transit Disease
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other Recommended: Avelumab. Useful in Certain Circumstances: Cytotoxic chemotherapy (Carboplatin, Cisplatin, combinations with Etoposide, CAV, Topotecan), Ipilimumab, Octreotide LAR (if somatostatin receptor positive), Pazopanib, T-VEC for palliation [MCC-D 3 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Octreotide LAR requires positive somatostatin receptor testing.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 2A recommendations for ICIs as standard of care [MCC-D 4 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other Recommended: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (particularly for disease refractory to PD-1 therapy [footnote g]). Useful in Certain Circumstances: Cytotoxic chemotherapy if anti-PD-L1 or anti-PD-1 therapy is contraindicated or progressed; Tumor NGS may reveal actionable targets [footnote g]; Octreotide LAR, Pazopanib, T-VEC are category 2B in this setting [MCC-D 4 of 5].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Octreotide LAR requires positive somatostatin receptor testing.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Adjuvant systemic therapy is not routinely recommended as survival benefit has not been demonstrated in retrospective studies. Could be used on case-by-case basis if clinical judgment dictates. No data to support adjuvant use of immunotherapy outside a clinical trial [MCC-D 3 of 5, footnote e].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant Therapy for Localized Disease (Clinical N0)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Adjuvant systemic therapy is not recommended outside of a clinical trial [MCC-D 2 of 5].

key_regimens.name: Avelumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: Continued until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Infusion-related reactions (common), fatigue, diarrhea, rash, nausea. Immune-related AEs occur in <20% of patients, typically grade 1-2 [MS-16].

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: Up to 2 years (per standard protocol for MCC from trials).
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related AEs (fatigue, rash, diarrhea, pruritus, hepatitis, colitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies). Treatment-related AEs in 68-77% of patients, grade 3/4 in 5-21% [MS-16]. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph (SC) may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab [MCC-D, footnote d].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: Varies by trial; often continued for 2 years.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Similar to pembrolizumab. ORR 71% in treatment-naïve MCC [MS-17]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy (SC) may be substituted for IV nivolumab [MCC-D, footnote c].

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab (Neoadjuvant)
key_regimens.setting: Locally advanced, surgically resectable (to convert to resectable)

key_regimens.name: Retifanlimab-dlwr

drugs.name: Retifanlimab-dlwr
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks (4 induction), then 1000 mg every 6 weeks

key_regimens.cycle_days: Induction phase: Q3W x 4 doses, then maintenance Q6W.

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks x 4 cycles (ipilimumab), then nivolumab maintenance.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3/4 TRAEs 40% in cohort A of phase 2 trial. Particularly indicated for disease refractory to PD-1 therapy [MS-17; MCC-D, footnote g].

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Etoposide
key_regimens.setting: Recurrent regional or disseminated disease (if anti-PD-1/PD-L1 contraindicated or progressed)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), nausea, alopecia, fatigue. Short-lived responses (median 2-9 months), higher risk of toxic death in older patients [MS-18].

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Etoposide
key_regimens.setting: Recurrent regional or disseminated disease (if anti-PD-1/PD-L1 contraindicated or progressed)

key_regimens.name: CAV
key_regimens.setting: Recurrent regional or disseminated disease (if anti-PD-1/PD-L1 contraindicated or progressed)

key_regimens.name: Talimogene Laherparepvec (T-VEC)
key_regimens.setting: In-transit or injectable sites (palliation of injectable lesions)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Until complete response of all injectable lesions, no new lesions, or for up to 12 months.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Local injection site reactions (pain, swelling, ulceration), flu-like symptoms, fatigue.

key_regimens.name: Octreotide Long-Acting Release (LAR)
key_regimens.setting: Disseminated disease (category 2B), recurrent regional disease (if somatostatin receptor positive)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: GI disturbances, hyperglycemia, gallstones. Demonstrated disease stabilization in some patients [MS-19].

key_regimens.name: Pazopanib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, diarrhea, fatigue, hepatotoxicity. Some disease stabilization reported in case series [MS-20].

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response and Surveillance
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment timing varies by treatment type and is generally performed after completing neoadjuvant therapy, periodically during systemic therapy (e.g., every 8-12 weeks), and during follow-up surveillance.
response_logic: For patients receiving neoadjuvant nivolumab, response is assessed clinically and radiographically. Adequate response permits proceeding to surgery [MCC-3].
During systemic therapy for advanced/metastatic disease, response is typically assessed using RECIST criteria via imaging.

Serum MCPyV oncoprotein antibody titers and/or ctDNA levels can serve as surrogate markers of disease burden and treatment response, often trending downwards with effective treatment [MCC-1, footnote c, d].
imaging_recommendations: Baseline staging: Whole-body FDG-PET/CT (preferred) or CT with contrast [MCC-1, footnote b].

Surveillance imaging: Typically via diagnostic CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Neck CT included if primary was head/neck. Whole-body FDG-PET/CT may be indicated to evaluate for in-transit metastases if primary lesion was on extremity [MCC-6, footnote v].
Imaging is performed as clinically indicated for symptoms or rising biomarkers.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: A biopsy should be considered to confirm suspected recurrence or progression, especially if changing treatment strategy.

For suspected recurrence, re-staging with comprehensive imaging is required to determine if disease is local, regional, or distant, which dictates salvage therapy per the appropriate pathway (MCC-2, MCC-3, MCC-4, MCC-5) [MCC-6].

prognosis.overall: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is one of the most aggressive skin cancers, with an incidence of approximately 2,488 individuals affected per year in the United States [1]. The disease can grow rapidly and metastasize early, with 63% of primary lesions having grown rapidly in the 3 months prior to diagnosis [7]. Large meta-analyses have shown that at least half of patients with MCC develop lymph node metastases and nearly one-third develop distant metastases [8-15]. Several studies document the development of locoregional recurrence in up to half of all instances of MCC [14-20]. MCC has a high mortality rate, exceeding melanoma. The 5-year relative or MCC-specific survival rates range from 41% to 77%, depending on stage at presentation [2,10,13,18-23].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 5-year relative survival ranges from 41% to 77% depending on stage (overall range across all stages); patients with clinical stage I/II MCC treated with surgery ± adjuvant RT had median OS of 76 months in the largest comparative study [199]
by_stage.context: Stage I includes T1 primary tumors (maximum clinical diameter ≤2 cm) with no nodal or distant metastasis

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 5-year relative survival ranges from 41% to 77% depending on specific stage subgroup [2,10,13,18-23]; patients with stage I/II disease treated with definitive RT vs surgery had median OS of 25 vs 76 months (P < .001) [199]
by_stage.context: Stage IIA includes T2-T3 (tumor >2 but ≤5 cm or >5 cm), Stage IIB includes T4 (tumor invades fascia, muscle, cartilage, or bone)

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Patients with stage III disease treated with surgery vs definitive RT had median OS of 30 vs 15 months (P < .001) [199]
by_stage.context: Stage III includes regional nodal involvement or in-transit metastases; Stage IIIA = occult nodal disease found on SLNB, Stage IIIB = clinically detected nodal disease or in-transit metastases

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 5-year OS rate of 26% (95% CI, 17-36) for patients with previously treated mMCC receiving avelumab [285]; 4-year OS rate of 38% (95% CI, 29-47) for first-line avelumab [288]
by_stage.context: Stage IV includes distant metastases (M1a: distant skin/SC tissue/LNs; M1b: lung; M1c: other visceral sites)

prognostic_factors: Lymphovascular invasion (LVI): LVI is predictive of SLN positivity, recurrence, OS, and DSS. A large review (N=500) showed <1% versus 35% of those who died of MCC were LVI-negative and LVI-positive, respectively [22,72,75-78]

Tumor size and thickness: Large database analyses have found primary tumor size significantly associated with nodal involvement, DSS, and OS [11,13,49,68,79,82]. Risk of microscopic LN involvement is non-negligible even among patients in the smallest tumor size category [13,49,77,82,83]

Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs): Presence of TILs associated with improved survival outcomes. A retrospective cohort (N=2182) established that subdivision into nonbrisk and brisk TILs was associated with incrementally improved OS compared with no TILs [76,85-91]
Tumor growth pattern: Infiltrative growth pattern associated with higher risk of SLN positivity or poor outcomes compared with nodular pattern [76,83]

Immunosuppression: MCC is disproportionally more common in immunosuppressed individuals (organ transplant, CLL, HIV). MCC-specific survival is worse for those with immunosuppression in multiple studies [19,38,40-47,114]

Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) status: MCPyV-negative tumors tend to occur more often on the head, neck, or trunk and have been associated with increased recurrence [55] and decreased MCC-specific survival and OS in some studies [57-60]. Genetic analyses found much higher mutational burden in MCPyV-negative tumors [63-65]

Margin status: In the largest study (N=6901, NCDB), margin status was significantly associated with survival for MCC with stage I, II, or III [68]. Among those with stage I/II disease treated with surgery only, recurrence rates and OS were better for negative versus positive margins [205]
Age and sex: MCC incidence increases with age and is more likely to occur in white individuals compared with other ethnicities [4,7,8,13,19]

Extensive lymph node involvement: Prognosis worsens with increasing nodal involvement including clinically detectable vs microscopic, increasing number of nodes, and presence of extranodal extension [12,13,17,22,49,68,72,79,82,181,215,228,239,258,259]
Head/neck primary site: Identified as an adverse risk factor for local recurrence [19,22,38,40-47,49,72,75-78,114]
Secondary malignancy risk: Patients with MCC are at higher risk for developing second cancers including SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, NHLs, lung, breast, and kidney cancers [19,23,35,91,94,97-104]
prognosis.prognostic_context: MCC prognosis is stage-dependent and influenced by multiple factors including immune status, viral etiology (MCPyV positive vs negative), histopathological features (LVI, TILs, growth pattern), and treatment received. The disease demonstrates early metastatic potential with rapid growth kinetics. Multidisciplinary management at specialized centers is recommended given the complexity of prognostic assessment and treatment planning.

disease_related.management: Up to half of all MCC cases develop locoregional recurrence [14-20]. Median time to recurrence is about 8 to 9 months, with 90% of recurrences occurring within 24 months [17,18,48,138]. Locoregional recurrence is associated with development of distant metastasis and poorer DSS and OS. Treatment options include clinical trial enrollment (preferred), systemic therapy, RT, or surgery [MCC-6]

Nearly one-third develop distant metastases [8-15]. Most likely sites include distant lymph nodes or skin, bone/bone marrow, lung/pleura, liver, followed by pancreas, adrenal glands, brain, kidneys, subcutaneous tissue, or muscle [16,72,101,102,138,277-279]. Treatment includes systemic therapy, RT, surgery in highly selective circumstances, or best supportive care [MCC-5]

Patients with MCC are at higher risk for prior, concurrent, or subsequent second primary malignancies including SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, NHLs, lung, breast, and kidney cancers [19,23,35,91,94,97-104]. Skin self-examination education is important [MCC-6]

chemotherapy: Treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) occurring in 68%-77% of patients receiving systemic therapy
Grade 3 or 4 AEs occurring in 5%-21% with checkpoint immunotherapies
Reported rates of toxic death with chemotherapy between 3% and 10%, with patients who are older being at higher risk [234,277,306]
Platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin/cisplatin with etoposide) carries risks of nephrotoxicity, myelosuppression, and ototoxicity
CAV regimen (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin/epirubicin, vincristine) carries risks of cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin) and neurotoxicity (vincristine)
immunotherapy: Treatment-related adverse events (TRAEs) similar for avelumab, pembrolizumab, and nivolumab with rates of 68%-77%
Immune-related AEs seen in <20% of patients receiving avelumab, all grade 1 or 2 [282-287,293,295-297]
Safety profiles for checkpoint immunotherapies are significantly different from cytotoxic therapies; clinician and patient education is critical for safe administration [MCC-D]
Ipilimumab + nivolumab: Grade 3 or 4 TRAEs observed in 40% (cohort A) and 32% (cohort B) in the randomized phase 2 trial [291]
TRAE incidence higher for combination ipilimumab + nivolumab than nivolumab monotherapy (47% vs 28%) [292]
Checkpoint inhibitor-related toxicities require monitoring per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities
targeted_therapy: Pazopanib: No unusual toxicities reported in a case series of 5 patients [319]; generally well tolerated with disease stabilization in 4 patients
Somatostatin analogs (octreotide LAR): Generally well tolerated; disease stabilization achieved in 47% of patients in a phase II study [316]
surgery: Surgical margins should be balanced with morbidity of surgery to achieve primary tissue closure and avoid undue delay to adjuvant RT [MCC-C]
Potential for wound healing complications, especially when close to critical structures (eye, mouth)
Reconstruction involving extensive tissue movement should be delayed until negative histologic margins verified and SLNB performed if indicated [MCC-C]
SLNB itself carries risks including lymphedema (particularly with groin nodal basin), wound infection, and seroma formation

In-transit disease: Hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion (HILP) carries procedure-specific risks; complete responses reported in 86% of patients but with rates of locoregional recurrence and distant metastasis [315]
radiation: Radiation treatments should be given by a practicing radiation oncologist with radiation physics support [MCC-B]
Application of bolus over the primary tumor should be considered; use of surface dosimeters recommended to verify delivered dose [MCC-B]
Adjuvant RT delays >7 to 8 weeks associated with worse outcomes [240,241]
Hypofractionation should be avoided in higher risk patients due to limited data [MCC-B]
Caution using multiple fractions >5 Gy/fraction when targets involve cartilage or bone [MCC-B]
Lymphedema risk, particularly for patients with existing lymphedema or at high risk (high BMI, groin nodal basin, current smoker) [MCC-B]
Skin toxicity (radiation dermatitis) is expected during and after treatment

supportive_care.overview: MCC patients require comprehensive multidisciplinary supportive care addressing the physical, psychological, and practical challenges of treatment. As MCC is one of the most aggressive skin cancers, supportive care must be integrated throughout the treatment continuum. Key considerations include managing immunosuppression, treatment-related toxicities from both immunotherapy and cytotoxic chemotherapy, prevention and management of recurrence, and addressing the psychosocial burden of a rare and aggressive malignancy. Patients who are immunocompromised require modification or reduction of immunosuppression as clinically feasible, as immunosuppression in MCC is a risk factor for poor outcomes [19,38,40-47,49,114].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: No specific nutritional guidance is provided in the NCCN MCC guidelines. However, general supportive nutritional care principles should apply for patients receiving chemotherapy or radiation therapy. Nutritional assessment should be part of multidisciplinary care.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: No MCC-specific antiemetic protocols are detailed in the NCCN MCC guidelines. Standard antiemetic regimens applicable to the specific chemotherapy agents used (platinum-based regimens, CAV, topotecan) should be followed per institutional protocols and relevant NCCN supportive care guidelines.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: No specific G-CSF guidance is provided within the MCC guidelines. G-CSF support should follow institutional and NCCN neutropenia guidelines based on the chemotherapy regimen and individual patient risk factors.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: No MCC-specific venous thromboembolosis (VTE) prophylaxis recommendations are provided in the guidelines. Standard VTE prophylaxis protocols should apply based on the clinical setting and patient risk factors.
supportive_care.pain_management: No specific pain management protocols are detailed in the MCC guidelines. General oncologic pain management principles should be applied based on disease burden, treatment setting, and individual patient needs. Palliative care should be considered as appropriate, with referral per NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: No MCC-specific psychosocial support protocols are detailed in the guidelines. Given the aggressive nature of MCC, high recurrence rates, and the psychological impact of a rare skin cancer diagnosis, psychosocial support should be integrated into multidisciplinary care. Patient advocacy organizations and counseling services should be made available.
supportive_care.dental_care: No specific dental care recommendations are provided within the MCC guidelines. Standard dental care considerations for patients receiving chemotherapy (e.g., dental evaluation prior to platinum-based chemotherapy) should be followed per institutional protocols.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: The NCCN Panel recommends close clinical follow-up for patients with MCC starting immediately after diagnosis and treatment. Physical examination should include a complete skin and complete lymph node (LN) examination every 3-6 months for the first 3 years, then every 6-12 months thereafter. The recommended frequency of follow-up visits is purposely broad to allow for an individualized schedule based on the risk of recurrence, stage of disease, and other factors such as patient anxiety and clinician preference. The Panel's recommendation for frequent clinical exams during the first 3 years reflects that MCC will recur in up to half of patients, and most recurrences occur within the first few years after diagnosis [17,18,48,138]. Education regarding self-examination of the skin is useful for patients with MCC because of their increased risk for other nonmelanoma skin cancers (NMSCs) [MCC-6].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Surveillance is critical because recurrence and development of lymph node and distant metastases in MCC are not uncommon [14-19,84,226,320]. Based on data from large retrospective analyses (N > 100), the median time to recurrence in patients with MCC is about 8 to 9 months, with 90% of recurrences occurring within 24 months [17,18,48,138]. Time to local recurrence is generally shorter than for regional recurrence, and time to distant metastasis is longer [16,17,19,225]. Due to the fast-growing nature of the disease, detection of multiple distant metastases at once is not uncommon [125]. Multiple retrospective analyses have shown that recurrence of MCC is associated with poor prognosis [71,73,93,239,252,312], and locoregional recurrence is associated with the development of distant metastasis.
late_effects_screening: Second primary malignancies: Patients with MCC are at higher risk for developing second cancers including SCC, BCC, melanoma, CLL, NHLs, lung, breast, and kidney cancers [19,23,35,91,94,97-104]
Late effects of radiation therapy including fibrosis, lymphedema (particularly with groin nodal basin irradiation), and secondary malignancies in radiation fields
Late effects of immunotherapy including endocrinopathies and other immune-related adverse events
Chronic immunosuppression-related complications in patients with ongoing immunosuppressive conditions
Lymphedema surveillance in patients who underwent lymph node dissection or regional radiation
Dermatologic surveillance for NMSC given increased risk of second skin cancers
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Based on data from large retrospective analyses, the median time to recurrence is about 8 to 9 months, with 90% of recurrences occurring within 24 months [17,18,48,138]. Locoregional recurrence is associated with the development of distant metastasis, and all types of recurrences (local, regional, and distant) may be associated with poorer DSS and OS [71,73,93,239,252,312]. Detection of multiple distant metastases at once is not uncommon [125]. Patients who have had MCC are at increased risk for a prior, concurrent, or subsequent second primary malignancy [23,35,97-100]. Risk factors for recurrence include immunosuppression, advancing age, advancing stage of disease (stage II-IV), non-SLN metastases, Merkel Cell polyomavirus negative status, as well as additional factors as determined by the treating physicians [20,McEvoy AM, et al. J Am Acad Dermatol 2024;90:569-576], [MCC-6]

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Physical exam including complete skin and complete LN exam: Every 3-6 months for 3 years; every 6-12 months thereafter [MCC-6]
Imaging and other studies as clinically indicated [MCC-6]
Immunocompromised patients: more frequent follow-up may be indicated [MCC-6, footnote e]
Self-examination of the skin recommended for patients with MCC due to increased risk for NMSCs [MCC-6]
imaging_strategy: Surveillance imaging typically via diagnostic CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis with oral and IV contrast [MCC-6, footnote v]
Neck CT often included if primary lesion was on head/neck [MCC-6, footnote v]
Whole-body FDG-PET/CT may be indicated to evaluate for in-transit metastases if the primary lesion is on the extremity [MCC-6, footnote v]
Routine imaging surveillance recommended for patients at high risk, including stage IIIB or higher and immunosuppression [MCC-6]

A study of 61 patients with stage III MCC who were clinically asymptomatic and underwent surveillance FDG-PET/CT revealed a recurrence rate of 33%, with sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy of 92%, 93%, and 93%, respectively [323]
laboratory_monitoring: Serum MCPyV oncoprotein antibodies: quantitation may be considered as part of initial workup; for surveillance often obtained every 3 months. Patients who test seronegative have a higher risk of recurrence; in patients who test seropositive, a rising titer may be an early indicator of recurrence. Baseline testing should be performed within 3 months of treatment because titers are expected to decrease significantly after clinically evident disease is eliminated [MCC-1, footnote c; 55,56,117-119]

Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA): can assess disease burden in both virus-positive and virus-negative MCC and typically becomes positive prior to or at the time of a clinically evident recurrence. For surveillance, this test is often obtained every 3 months [MCC-1, footnote d; 120-122]
Laboratory studies as clinically indicated (e.g., complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, liver function tests) [MCC-1]
supportive_follow_up: Immunosuppressive treatments should be minimized as clinically feasible in consultation with the relevant managing physician [MCC-1, footnote e]
Education regarding self-examination of the skin [MCC-6]
Multidisciplinary consultation recommended at center with specialized expertise [MCC-1 through MCC-6]
Consider genetic counseling to evaluate for germline mutations in cancer-associated genes in patients <50 years [MCC-1, footnote f]

special_situations.key_message: Immunosuppression in MCC is a risk factor for poor outcomes. MCC is disproportionally more common in immunosuppressed individuals such as those with organ transplants, CLL, or HIV infections. MCC-specific survival is worse for those with immunosuppression.
special_situations.management: Immunosuppressive treatments should be minimized as clinically feasible in consultation with the relevant managing physician. As patients who are immunocompromised are at high risk for recurrence, more frequent follow-up may be indicated. In patients with well-controlled HIV, the infection appears to respond to PD-1 pathway blockade at a rate similar to patients without immune compromise [304]. Neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered with caution in immunocompromised surgical candidates.
special_situations.evidence: Multiple retrospective studies document worse MCC-specific survival with immunosuppression [19,38,40-47]. Case series of 3 HIV-associated MCC patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy showed favorable responses [304]. [MCC-1, footnote e]

special_situations.key_message: Mutations in known cancer-associated genes that may be heritable have been reported in approximately 20% of patients with MCC under 50 years of age.
special_situations.management: Consider genetic counseling to evaluate for germline mutations in cancer-associated genes in patients <50 years of age [MCC-1, footnote f].
special_situations.evidence: Mohsin N, et al. JAMA Dermatol 2024;160:172-178 [116]

special_situations.key_message: Among patients with clinically apparent nodal disease at presentation, those with unknown primary have a better outcome than those with synchronous known primary. NCCN recommendations for pathologic evaluation of nodal status and management of the nodal basin are the same for both groups.
special_situations.management: The primary tumor can be treated following the resectable local MCC or locally advanced MCC pathways. SLNB should be performed prior to surgical removal of the primary tumor with special care in head and neck region where drainage patterns are complex. Management of draining nodal basin and in-transit disease follow separate recommendations.
special_situations.evidence: Studies document better outcomes in unknown primary MCC compared to synchronous known primary [12,41,175-178]. These findings are reflected in the AJCC staging system [66].

special_situations.situation: Locally advanced MCC (curative surgery and RT not feasible)
special_situations.key_message: For locally advanced MCC patients who are surgical candidates but for whom curative surgery and RT are not feasible, multidisciplinary consultation at a specialized center is recommended. Neoadjuvant immunotherapy is the favored treatment option.
special_situations.management: Neoadjuvant nivolumab may be considered for patients who are surgical candidates. If adequate response is observed, patients become eligible for standard resectable local MCC treatment. If progression occurs on neoadjuvant nivolumab, discuss RT or surgery for local control versus systemic therapy. For nonsurgical candidates, discuss RT for durable local control versus systemic therapy.
special_situations.evidence: Category 2A recommendation based on lower-level evidence with uniform NCCN consensus. [MCC-3, MCC-D]

special_situations.key_message: In-transit disease is defined as discontinuous from primary tumor, located between the primary tumor and the draining regional nodal basin, or distal to the primary tumor. Treatment options differ from regionally nodal positive MCC.
special_situations.management: Diagnosis confirmation with biopsy is required. Treatment options include clinical trials (preferred), surgery, radiation, systemic therapy, and additional local considerations (T-VEC for palliation of injectable sites, hyperthermic isolated limb infusion/perfusion) if curative surgery and/or RT are not feasible.
special_situations.evidence: These recommendations are based on expert opinion due to lack of high-quality peer-reviewed primary literature [247-250]. [MCC-4]

special_situations.key_message: Patients with well-controlled HIV were not represented in initial checkpoint inhibitor trials; however, the infection appears to respond to PD-1 pathway blockade at a rate similar to patients without immune compromise.
special_situations.management: Immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy (PD-1/PD-L1 blockade) can be considered. Avelumab, nivolumab, and pembrolizumab are listed as preferred systemic therapy options. Close monitoring for both immune-related adverse events and HIV-related complications is recommended.
special_situations.evidence: Case series of 3 patients: two of three responded to PD-1 pathway blockade [304]. [MCC-D]

special_situations.key_message: Metastatic MCC commonly expresses somatostatin receptors (SSTRs), making somatostatin analog therapy a potential treatment option.
special_situations.management: Octreotide long-acting release (LAR) may be considered if somatostatin receptor testing is positive. Listed as 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' for recurrent regional and disseminated disease.
special_situations.evidence: Phase II study (N=58): 3% partial response, disease stabilization for at least 6 months in 47% (N=27) [316]. Retrospective study (N=39 with SSTR-positive MCC, N=19 treated with SSA): 43% disease control with median PFS of 237 days [317]. [MCC-D]

clinical_pearls: MCC can grow rapidly and metastasize early: 63% of primary lesions have grown rapidly in the 3 months prior to diagnosis, and up to half of patients develop lymph node metastases [7,8-15].

Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is an essential staging tool: SLNB should be performed prior to or at the time of definitive excision, regardless of surgical approach. Rates of SLN positivity range from 30%-40% in clinically node-negative disease [19,46,49,94,127,180,181].

Imaging is encouraged for baseline staging: Occult metastatic disease resulting in upstaging has been detected in 12%-20% of patients presenting without suspicious H&P findings. Whole-body FDG-PET/CT is preferred at initial workup [151].

Immunosuppression is a critical adverse prognostic factor: MCC-specific survival is worse for immunosuppressed patients. Minimize immunosuppressive treatments as clinically feasible [19,38,40-47,114].

Checkpoint immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1 blockade) has transformed MCC treatment: Durable responses are improved with PD-1/PD-L1 blockade compared with cytotoxic therapy. Avelumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, and retifanlimab-dlwr are preferred systemic therapy options [MCC-D].
Adjuvant radiation therapy delay >7-8 weeks is associated with worse outcomes: RT should be initiated as soon as wound healing permits [240,241].

MCPyV oncoprotein antibodies and ctDNA are emerging surveillance biomarkers: Seronegative patients have higher recurrence risk; rising titers may indicate recurrence. ctDNA typically becomes positive prior to or at the time of clinical recurrence [55,121,122].
Ipilimumab + nivolumab combination is particularly indicated for disease refractory to PD-1 therapy, with an objective response rate of 31% in ICI-pretreated patients [22,291].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Merkel Cell Carcinoma, Version 2.2026
guidelines_resources.notes: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated. NCCN recognizes the importance of clinical trials and encourages participation when applicable and available. Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise is recommended for all patients.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for detection and management of immune-related adverse events associated with checkpoint immunotherapies [MCC-D].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for best supportive care recommendations for patients with disseminated disease [MCC-5, MCC-6].

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017)
guidelines_resources.notes: Staging system for MCC (8th ed., 2017) based on analysis of 9387 patients from NCDB with median follow-up of 28.2 months [12]. [ST-1, ST-2]

guidelines_resources.title: Protocol for the Examination of Specimens from Patients with Merkel Cell Carcinoma of the Skin, Version 4.0.0.1
guidelines_resources.notes: Synoptic reporting protocol preferred for MCC pathology specimens [67].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Squamous Cell Skin Cancer - Principles of PDEMA Technique
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for description of peripheral and deep en face margin assessment (PDEMA) technique used in Mohs and other surgical approaches [MCC-2, footnote j].

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines Index
guidelines_resources.notes: Comprehensive index for navigating NCCN guidelines including Merkel Cell Carcinoma.

key_trials.full_name: Avelumab in patients with chemotherapy-refractory metastatic Merkel cell carcinoma
key_trials.population: Stage IV distant MCC progressing after ≥1 prior line of chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS 12.6 months (95% CI, 7.5-17.1); 5-year OS rate 26% (95% CI, 17-36)
key_trials.practice_change: Established avelumab as effective second-line therapy for mMCC

key_trials.full_name: First-line avelumab in patients with metastatic Merkel cell carcinoma
key_trials.key_result: ORR 39.7% (95% CI, 30.7-49.2); median PFS 4.1 months (95% CI, 1.4-6.1); median OS 20.3 months (95% CI, 12.4-NE); 4-year OS rate 38% (95% CI, 29-47)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PD-L1+ median OS 38.7 months; PD-L1- median OS 16.1 months [288]
key_trials.practice_change: Established avelumab as preferred first-line therapy for metastatic MCC

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant nivolumab for patients with resectable Merkel cell carcinoma
key_trials.population: Resectable MCC stage IIA-IV who received ≥1 dose of nivolumab
key_trials.key_result: pCR in 47.2% (N=17/36); 54.5% (N=18/33) had tumor reductions ≥30%. Median RFS and OS not reached at median follow-up of 20.3 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Recurrence-free survival and overall survival not reached [293]
key_trials.practice_change: Established neoadjuvant nivolumab as useful option for resectable MCC

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab in patients with virus-associated tumors - MCC cohort
key_trials.key_result: ORR 71% (95% CI, 42-92) in treatment-naïve; ORR 63% (95% CI, 25-92) in previously treated
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: CR 21% vs 0% in treatment-naïve vs previously treated [294]
key_trials.practice_change: Supported nivolumab monotherapy as systemic therapy option for advanced MCC

key_trials.full_name: PD-1 blockade with pembrolizumab in advanced Merkel-cell carcinoma
key_trials.journal: N Engl J Med / J Clin Oncol / J Immunother Cancer
key_trials.population: Distant metastatic or locoregional MCC not amenable to definitive surgery or RT, no prior systemic therapy
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab as preferred first-line therapy for advanced MCC

key_trials.full_name: Phase 2 study of retifanlimab in patients with advanced or metastatic MCC
key_trials.population: Unresectable laMCC or mMCC, chemotherapy-naïve or chemotherapy-refractory, no prior anti-PD-1/L1
key_trials.key_result: ORR 46.2% (N=30/65 assessed); CR 12.3%, PR 33.8%; disease control rate 53.8%
key_trials.practice_change: Established retifanlimab-dlwr as preferred therapy option

key_trials.full_name: Combined nivolumab and ipilimumab with or without SBRT for advanced Merkel cell carcinoma
key_trials.population: Advanced MCC, including ICI-pretreated and ICI-naïve
key_trials.key_result: ICI-naïve (N=22): ORR 100% (95% CI, 82-100), CR 41%. ICI-pretreated (N=26): ORR 31% (95% CI, 15-52), CR 15%. No significant difference between cohorts with/without SBRT (P=0.26)
key_trials.practice_change: Established ipilimumab + nivolumab as option particularly for PD-1-refractory MCC

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab with or without ipilimumab in patients with recurrent or metastatic MCC
key_trials.key_result: Nivolumab: ORR 60%, median DOR 60.6 months, median PFS 21.3 months, OS 80.7 months. Nivo+Ipi: ORR 58%, median DOR 25.9 months, median PFS 8.4 months, OS 29.8 months
key_trials.practice_change: Compared nivolumab monotherapy vs combination for MCC

key_trials.full_name: Real-world clinical outcomes with first-line avelumab in laMCC/mMCC in the USA
key_trials.population: mMCC (N=19) and laMCC (N=9) treated with first-line avelumab
key_trials.key_result: mMCC: median OS 20.2 months (95% CI, 10.0-NR), PFS 10 months, response rate 63.2%. laMCC: median OS and PFS not reached, response rate 66.7%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Real-world outcomes consistent with clinical trial data [281]
key_trials.practice_change: Confirmed real-world effectiveness of first-line avelumab
